lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'Network Attached Storage is a growing market with on-premises and cloud NAS solutions. SMB and enterprise NAS actively developed by NetApp, Synology, QNAP, Dell, and cloud providers.', 'source': 'General knowledge'}
notes: Core product category; Snap Appliance positioned as #1 volume NAS provider

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'legacy-supported', 'notes': 'ATA/IDE disk interface (IDE/ATA-100) is legacy but parallel ATA drives still exist in some legacy systems. SATA largely superseded parallel ATA. The ATA-100 interface specifically is discontinued in new hardware.', 'source': 'General storage technology evolution'}
notes: Snap Appliances use inexpensive ATA disks in redundancy configurations; ATA-100 disks in 4200/4500 departmental products

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'Snap Server 1100 is an early-generation NAS appliance (circa 2002-2004). Overland Tandberg/Overland Storage discontinued multiple SnapServer models. DX1/DX2 EOL was December 31, 2018.', 'source': 'https://www.parkplacetechnologies.com/eosl/family/snapserver/'}
notes: World best-selling NAS server at time of study; capable and portable file server; desktop form factor

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'Snap Server 2200 is a legacy NAS model from Snap Appliance. The company was acquired by Adaptec, then by Overland Storage. Early SnapServer models like 2200 are long discontinued.', 'source': 'https://www.parkplacetechnologies.com/eosl/family/snapserver/'}
notes: Larger storage capacity plus RAID 1 data protection; workgroup tier

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'Snap Server 4100 is a legacy NAS model. The product line from the 2003-era Snap Appliance era has been discontinued. SnapServer 110 discontinued January 2011.', 'source': 'https://www.backupworks.com/snapserver-110-discontinued-REO-1550.aspx'}

{'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'Snap Server 4200 is a legacy NAS model from the early Snap Appliance era. Discontinued along with the broader early SnapServer product line.', 'source': 'https://www.parkplacetechnologies.com/eosl/family/snapserver/'}
notes: Departmental; 4x ATA-100 disks; total capacity up to 640GB; 1U rack with dual gigabit Ethernet

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'Snap Server 4500 is a legacy NAS model from the early Snap Appliance era. Discontinued as part of the overall early SnapServer product line discontinuation.', 'source': 'https://www.parkplacetechnologies.com/eosl/family/snapserver/'}
notes: Departmental; up to 1TB; supports up to two Snap Disk 10 expansion arrays; up to 3TB total; all RAID options

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'SnapServer 14000 was a mid-range NAS appliance. The SnapServer DX1 and DX2 (later models) reached EOSL December 31, 2018; the older 14000 was discontinued earlier.', 'source': 'https://www.parkplacetechnologies.com/eosl/family/snapserver/'}
notes: Enterprise-class; 1.44TB / 2.16TB / 3.0TB; 12 disks; 3U rack; ADS and NIS security protocol support

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'Snap Disk 10 Expansion Array was an accessory for early Snap Appliance NAS systems. Discontinued along with the associated SnapServer product lines.', 'source': 'https://www.parkplacetechnologies.com/eosl/family/snapserver/'}
notes: External storage expansion for Snap Server 4500; enables up to 3TB raw storage capacity

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'SnapOS was the original operating system for early Snap Server NAS devices. Replaced by GuardianOS. The original SnapOS brand is discontinued.', 'source': 'https://www.snaphq.com/Guardian-OS.asp'}
notes: Snap-developed OS for workgroup products; field-hardened; ease of use focus

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'GuardianOS (Linux-based NAS OS by Overland Storage for SnapServer) is listed as no longer available on the SnapHQ website. SnapServer DX1/DX2 EOL was December 2018.', 'source': 'https://www.snaphq.com/Guardian-OS.asp'}
notes: Linux-based OS for departmental and enterprise Snap Servers; adds snapshots and Windows ADS; same ease of use as SnapOS

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'DataKeeper Desktop Backup Software was a Snap Appliance product for desktop-to-NAS backup synchronization. The company and product line have been discontinued/absorbed.', 'source': 'General Snap Appliance product history'}
notes: Automated scheduled client backup; unlimited user license included with every Snap Server; Windows clients

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'evolved', 'notes': 'Server-to-server sync/replication software as a concept has evolved into modern data replication, DR, and cloud sync solutions (rsync, Veeam, NetApp SnapMirror, etc.). The specific Snap product is discontinued but the capability is active in modern form.', 'source': 'General storage technology evolution'}
notes: Replication between Snap Servers for data redundancy

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'RAID 1 (mirroring) remains widely used. As of 2025 RAID 1 represents 28% of Linux server RAID deployments. Standard data protection technique in servers, NAS, and storage arrays.', 'source': 'https://commandlinux.com/statistics/raid-configuration-usage-statistics-on-linux-servers/'}
notes: Offered in Snap Server 2200 for data protection

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'RAID 5 use has declined (dropped to 12% of Linux RAID deployments in 2025 from 30% in 2020) as organizations move to RAID 6/10 for large arrays. Still in active use, particularly for smaller arrays.', 'source': 'https://commandlinux.com/statistics/raid-configuration-usage-statistics-on-linux-servers/'}
notes: Offered from Snap Server 4100 upward; entry-level implementation in 4100

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'Gigabit Ethernet is mature and widely deployed. Now part of multi-gig standards (2.5G, 5G, 10GbE). Still the dominant LAN standard for endpoint connectivity.', 'source': 'General networking'}
notes: Dual gigabit Ethernet adapters in 1U rack-mounted 4200/4500 appliances

tech_name: Windows ADS / NIS Storage Security Protocols
lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'legacy-supported', 'notes': 'Windows ADS (Active Directory Services) remains active (now called Active Directory). notes: ADS (Active Directory Services) and NIS support in SnapServer 14000 for Unix/Windows datacenter environments
